name: Djagba et al. NGSS Classroom GPT Augmentation Extract
population_or_context: High school NGSS-aligned physical science assessment with student scientific explanations and 11 binary-coded analytic rubric categories.
sample_size: 1,466 original high school responses; GPT-generated positive responses were added for severely imbalanced categories, including 83 for Category 5, 150 for Category 6, 72 for Category 7, and enough Category 9 responses to reach about 140 positives.
methods: SciBERT classifiers were trained under original, fine-tuned, SMOTE, GPT-generated document-level augmentation, EASE, and ALP conditions using a fixed 80/20 train-test split.
education_context: High school science classroom assessment of NGSS-aligned scientific explanations using learning-progression analytic rubric categories.
response_origin: Real high school responses plus GPT-generated synthetic student responses for rare positive rubric categories.
rubric_label_space: Eleven binary-coded analytic categories covering scientific ideas and incomplete or inaccurate ideas.
label_source: Original responses were scored on analytic categories; generated responses were targeted to positive categories and evaluated through downstream classifier performance against held-out real responses.
generation_model: GPT-4 is reported as the document-level generation model; the PDF contains one internal example label that appears to say GPT-5, so model naming should be checked during review.
generation_method: Use few-shot prompting with the original item and example student answers for a target characteristic, then generate similar student responses to increase minority-class coverage.
prompt_strategy: Two-shot prompt with original item context, two student answer examples, student-role framing, and a request for new similar answers satisfying the analytic rubric criterion.
evaluation_method: Held-out 80/20 real-response test split with accuracy, precision, recall, and F1; discussion frames improved scoring as supporting feedback and learning progression placement.
scorer_use: Synthetic responses are used for training augmentation to address class imbalance in automated scoring.
real_response_comparator: The augmented models are evaluated on held-out real student responses using the same train-test split as original-data models.
reported_metrics: For Category 5, SB+GPT F1 = 86.80 compared with baseline F1 = 58.82; for Category 6, SB+GPT F1 = 91.20 compared with baseline F1 = 0.00. EASE and ALP often performed as well or better in the reported tables.
limitations: Conference preprint rather than an operational scoring policy or deployment study.
The assessment consequence is classroom feedback/learning-progression support; grade-bearing use is not established.
The authors state that synthetic data requires additional validity work, generalization studies, and expert quality checks.
GPT augmentation is one of several augmentation strategies and was not always the strongest method.
